title: The alternating square knot
subtitle: Staggered rows of square knots that build an open diamond lattice
excerpt: Alternating square knots are square knots tied in staggered rows, with each row offset by two cords from the row above. The result is an open diamond or mesh pattern. It is the most-used large-area structure in macramé: wall hangings, plant hangers, and bag bodies all rely on it for their characteristic open weave.
sourceNotes: The alternating square knot is a standard macramé structure. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pattern structure formed by tying square knots in rows, with each row offset by two cords from the row above. Adjacent rows share cords, creating an open diamond lattice.

The set of four cords (two working, two core) used for one square knot. Groupings are reassigned between rows in the alternating pattern: the right two cords of one group join the left two cords of the next group.

content.text: pattern builds from a simple rule: in the first row, tie square knots in

of four from left to right. In the second row, leave the two leftmost cords out and tie groups of four starting from cord 3. Third row returns to the first-row grouping. The offset between rows is consistent, and the open diamonds appear automatically once two rows are complete.

items.name: Cotton macramé cord, 3 mm single-strand
items.qty: 8 cords, each 120 cm (for a practice swatch)

content.text: Step 1: Mount cords and tie Row 1

Mount eight cords onto the dowel using lark's head knots. You now have 16 working ends. Starting from the left, tie a square knot using cords 1, 2, 3, 4 (1 and 4 are working cords; 2 and 3 are the core cords). Continue across: knot cords 5-8, then 9-12, then 13-16. Row 1 is four square knots.

Leave cords 1 and 2 hanging for now. Tie a square knot using cords 3, 4, 5, 6. Continue: knot cords 7-10, then 11-14. Leave cords 15 and 16 hanging. Row 2 is three square knots, offset by two cords. Leave a gap between rows of roughly 2 cm for a standard open mesh; a tighter gap produces denser mesh.
Row 3 returns to the original grouping: cords 1-4, 5-8, 9-12, 13-16. The diamonds should now be visible between rows 1 and 2. Continue alternating groupings for as many rows as needed.

The vertical gap between rows controls how open the mesh is. A 3 cm gap gives a loose, airy lattice. A 1 cm gap gives a compact mesh where the diamonds are more closed. Patterns usually specify the gap in centimetres; pin each row at the correct depth before tying if precision matters.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common errors when building alternating square knot panels.

items.symptom: Diamonds are uneven — some large, some small
items.cause: Row spacing was not kept consistent between first-row and second-row knots.
items.fix: Pin the mounting dowel and measure the drop to each row before tying. Consistent spacing produces even diamonds.

items.symptom: Edges pulling inward over several rows
items.cause: Edge cords are being used as working cords in one row and as core cords in another, compressing the edges.
items.fix: The two outermost cords (one at each edge) should only appear as working cords in the rows where they belong, never as core cords. Re-read the grouping before each row.
